data.title: Anglo American Q1 Copper Production Up 1%; Reaffirms Full-Year Guidance
data.description:
LONDON (dpa-AFX) - Anglo American plc (AAL.L) on Tuesday reported mixed production performance across its portfolio, with gains in copper, manganese and diamonds offset by declines in iron ore, coal and nickel.

Copper production rose 1% to 170,400 tonnes, supported by higher output at Los Bronces and Collahuasi due to increased throughput, partly offset by lower grades at Quellaveco.

Premium iron ore production fell 2% to 15.2 million tonnes, reflecting slightly lower output from Kumba and Minas-Rio.

Manganese ore production surged 118% to 759,100 tonnes.

Rough diamond production increased 17% to 7.1 million carats, driven by planned ore release from Gahcho Kué and higher volumes from Venetia underground.

Steelmaking coal production declined 31% to 1.5 million tonnes, mainly due to lower output at Moranbah North following the March 2025 incident and adverse weather at Dawson.

Nickel production decreased 7% to 9,100 tonnes.

For the full year, the company kept its production and unit cost guidance unchanged for continuing operations, with copper expected at 700-760 thousand tonnes, premium iron ore at 55-59 million tonnes, and diamonds at 21-26 million carats.

data.title: ZAWYA: PMI unveils the MENA construction talent gap report highlighting urgent workforce challenges
data.description:
Cairo, Egypt - The Project Management Institute (PMI) has released its latest report, “The Construction Project Management Talent Gap”, shedding light on a significant workforce shortage threatening the MENA region’s ability to deliver critical infrastructure projects by 2035. According to the report, the region is projected to require up to 330,794 construction project management professionals under a high-growth scenario by F2035, marking a 32% increase in projected demand compared to 2025 levels. However, the talent gap could leave MENA with a shortfall of 111,737 professionals, potentially stalling ambitious regional projects unless decisive actions are taken to address recruitment, development, and retention challenges.

As MENA countries continue to make transformative investments in infrastructure, energy transition, and sustainable urban development, including UAE’s smart cities, KSA’s Vision 2030, and Egypt’s mega-projects, bridging this talent gap will be vital to maintaining momentum and delivering projects on time and budget.

MENA’s Growing Demand for Skilled Professionals

PMI’s research highlights the urgent need for construction project talent to meet the region’s ambitious goals. Under a high-growth scenario, demand across MENA will increase by nearly 97,359 professionals by 2035, with additional attrition caused by retirements widening the gap further. A low-growth scenario still leaves the region short by over 88,561 professionals, emphasizing the importance of proactive strategies to overcome barriers.

Key challenges identified in the report include:

  • Perception Issues: Construction careers are often viewed as demanding and physically challenging, discouraging young talent from entering the industry. This perception issue needs urgent reframing to portray the innovation, dynamism, and rewarding opportunities in construction.
  • Gender Inclusion Gap: The sector has one of the lowest shares of women in the workforce globally, at only 24%, according to World Economic Forum data cited in the report. Fostering gender diversity can open up a considerable talent pool while promoting inclusive environments to attract and retain women professionals.
  • Training Limitations: The lack of structured career pathways, professional development opportunities, and access to certifications prevents entry-level and mid-career professionals from contributing fully to the workforce.
  • Lag in Technology Adoption: Emerging tools like Artificial Intelligence (AI), Building Information Modeling (BIM), and Digital Twins have massive potential to enhance workforce productivity, but construction organizations across MENA continue to lag in embracing digitalization compared to other industries.

Actionable Solutions to Close the Gap

PMI urges industry stakeholders in the MENA region to adopt forward-looking strategies to address the widening workforce gap. Investing in digital transformation tools, especially Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Building Information Modeling (BIM), are highlighted as critical enablers of productivity and retention while attracting younger generations who are already comfortable with technology. Structured career pathways, mentorship programs, and incentives for professional certifications are equally critical to developing and retaining talent. When employees see clear paths to progression and gain access to training opportunities, organizations foster stronger engagement and build a resilient workforce prepared for future challenges.

Promoting diversity and sustainability must also be central to workforce strategies. Breaking down barriers for women and other underrepresented groups in construction expands the talent pool while improving workplace culture and inclusivity. Additionally the report highlights rising demand for professionals with expertise in carbon management, impact assessment, and ESG-aligned (Environmental, Social, and Governance) practices. Strong communication and collaboration across project teams remain critical to minimizing delays and inefficiencies, ensuring smooth workflows and improved outcomes for the region’s ambitious infrastructure projects.

"The MENA construction sector is at a crossroads. Its ambitions, from revolutionary mega-projects to sustainable infrastructure, depend on bridging the growing talent gap effectively. Through proactive investments in recruitment, skills development, and inclusion, the region can transform this challenge into an opportunity to build a future-ready workforce” said Hanny Alshazly, Managing Director for Middle East and North Africa (MENA) at PMI. He adds, “At PMI, we are committed to empowering organizations with the tools and resources to close this gap, adopt agile approaches, and forge resilient paths to sustainable growth.”

The full report, which includes global insights and regional breakdowns, is now available for download at https://www.pmi.org/learning/thought-leadership/construction-project-management-talent-gap. PMI invites business leaders, policymakers, and industry stakeholders to explore the report’s findings and discover how PMI certifications and training programs can help address workforce challenges across the MENA construction landscape.

About PMI

PMI is the leading authority in project management, dedicated to guiding the way to project success. Since 1969, PMI has shone a light on the power of project management and the people behind the projects. With a global community, gold-standard professional certifications, and career-long learning opportunities, PMI empowers current and aspiring project professionals, as well as organizations, with knowledge and resources to lead effectively and create an impact in the communities they serve. Join PMI in elevating our world – one project at a time.

Join PMI in elevating our world – one project at a time. Connect with us at www.pmi.org, linkedin.com/company/projectmanagementinstitute, on Instagram @pmi_org, and on TikTok @PMInstitute.

PMI Trademarks

Project Management Institute and PMI are trademarks and/or registered marks of Project Management Institute, Inc., in the US and/or in other countries.

Third Party Trademarks

All other trademarks are the property of their respective owners.

Media Contact

Celine Nehme

Regional Communications Lead – MENA

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: Barclays Posts Higher Profit on Top-Line Growth
data.description:
By Elena Vardon

Barclays reported a rise in profit for the first quarter of 2026 on growth across most of its businesses and confirmed its annual and midterm targets.

The British bank on Tuesday posted a pretax profit of 2.81 billion pounds ($3.81 billion) for the three-month period, which was 3% higher than a year prior but slightly lower than the 2.83 billion-pound estimate from a company-compiled consensus.

The group's revenue came in at 8.16 billion pounds, slightly ahead of expectations of 8.12 billion pounds. It said the 6% on-year increase was driven by higher net interest income--what it earns on loans minus what it pays out on client deposits--thanks to loan growth and the hedging mechanisms in place to mitigate the impact of interest-rates moves, as well as higher fees from its global markets and investment banking divisions.

Barclays also set aside 823 million pounds to cover bad loans in the quarter, including a 228 million-pound charge driven by a single name in the investment bank.

"The breadth and quality of our businesses mean we remain confident in delivering all our financial targets across a range of environments," Chief Executive C. S. Venkatakrishnan said.

The bank intends to start buying back up to 500 million pounds of its own shares once its current 1 billion-pound program completes, it added.

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: ZAWYA: Jazeera Airways issues new travel instructions for Kuwait passengers
data.description:
Staff Writer

Kuwait - Jazeera Airways has issued updated travel instructions for passengers departing from Kuwait, outlining revised reporting points, transfer procedures, and timing requirements for both direct and connecting flights.

For direct flights from Kuwait, passengers must report to Jazeera Park & Fly at least 4 hours before departure. They are advised not to proceed directly to Kuwait International Airport Terminal 5. The airline said bus transfers will be provided from Jazeera Park & Fly to Terminal 5 for check-in and boarding.

The airline also clarified that parking is not permitted at either Jazeera Park & Fly or Terminal 5. However, valet parking services will be available at the Park & Fly facility for passenger convenience.

For flights operating via Dammam, passengers must assemble at Mishref Hall 8. Check-in at the assembly point will close 1 hour before the bus departs, and the bus to Dammam will depart 12 hours before the scheduled flight departure time.

The airline emphasized the importance of adhering to the revised timings to ensure smooth transfers and avoid delays.

data.title: London-listed Tullow Oil profit slumps on production hit, Ghana payment delays
data.description:
West Africa-focused independent oil and gas explorer Tullow Oil posted an 87% slump in annual profit on Tuesday, partly due to lower production, while delayed payments from the Ghana government continued to weigh on its finances.

The heavily indebted company had launched a capital overhaul after declining production and overdue payments from the Government of Ghana squeezed cash flow, while selling non‑core assets to streamline its operations around West Africa and steady the business.

The company now expects production to be at the higher end of the previously announced 34 kboepd-42 kboepd range, including about 6 kboepd of gas.

The London-listed oil producer said production averaged 40.4 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day (kboepd) in 2025, lower than 51.5 kboepd a year earlier, and added that production averaged 43.4 kboepd during the first quarter of 2026.

The London-listed company's profit after tax for the year ended December 31, 2025, came in at $7 million, compared with $55 million reported a year earlier.

data.title: BOJ's Mixed Forecasts Highlight Middle East Uncertainty — Market Talk
data.description:
0646 GMT - The BOJ's sharp upward revision to the inflation forecast and clear downgrade to the growth projection for the fiscal year ending March 2027 imply that the timing of further rate hikes remains heavily dependent on the situation in the Middle East, SMBC Nikko Securities economists say. In its quarterly report, the BOJ said "it will consider the timing and pace of adjustment, while closely monitoring the impact of the future course of the situation in the Middle East on Japan's economic activity and prices." The economists project that the BOJ will raise rates in June but caution that the central bank could delay if energy shortages worsen. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0644 GMT - The damage to government bond markets from the war in the Middle East could prove longer-lasting than that to equities, says Capital Economics in a note to clients. Most of the rise in yields this year reflects changes in expectations for monetary policy. The key question, therefore, is whether the war has had a lasting effect on the path for monetary policy, it adds. It appears as though the answer is yes, Capital Economics says. It's now unlikely the Fed or the Bank of England will cut this year, versus the expectation before the war. Current market pricing broadly supports the scenario. That suggests that a broad-based bond rally is unlikely even if the mood improves, it says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0643 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields rise in opening trade, tracking the direction of U.S. Treasurys. Tuesday's eurozone bond supply schedule is light, with only the Netherlands conducting an auction. The economic data calendar is moderate too before it picks up on Wednesday, though the European Central Bank's bank lending survey and consumer expectations survey are due for release. The 10-year German Bund yield rises 1.3 basis points to 3.049%, and yield increases on other countries' 10-year bonds is of approximately the same magnitude, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0639 GMT - The dollar rises after a U.S. official said Monday that President Trump is skeptical about Iran's latest proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and end the war. Trump sounded notes about Iran not dealing in good faith or being willing to meet his key demand of ending nuclear enrichment and vowing to never make a nuclear weapon, the WSJ reports. The latest news lifts oil prices, supporting the dollar as the U.S. is a net oil exporter. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 98.613. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0619 GMT - The Bank of Japan's policy decision was a "hawkish hold" that leaves the door open for a June rate hike, says Daiwa Securities economist Kento Minami. The BOJ upgraded its price projections in its quarterly report and said underlying inflation is expected to reach its target between the second half of fiscal 2026 and fiscal 2027. "The latest price outlook signals a heightened sense of vigilance regarding upside inflation risks," Minami says. "The hawkish tone of the outlook report underscores the BOJ's firm commitment to proceed with further interest rate hikes," he adds. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0609 GMT - The BOJ's hawkish rate hold is likely a "warning shot" to markets, Saxo Markets' Charu Chanana says in a commentary. "The Middle East shock is no longer just a geopolitical risk for markets; it is becoming a central bank risk," the chief investment strategist says. "If inflation pressure arrives before growth weakness becomes undeniable, policymakers may tighten or stay restrictive for longer than markets expect." This sustains upward pressure on short-dated bond yields, complicates the case for equities and makes the next round of global central bank meetings more important than usual, Chanana adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0604 GMT - The Bank of Japan appears increasingly wary of the risk of falling behind the curve on inflation, says Mitsubishi UFJ Morgan Stanley Securities strategist Naomi Muguruma. In its report issued Tuesday, the BOJ said it needs to avoid a spike in inflation that could cool the economy, given that underlying consumer inflation is approaching the 2% target and companies are more actively passing on higher costs to wages and prices. "It shows that even though the economy is slowing down [due to the impact of the Middle East conflict], there will be no major change to the BOJ's path toward achieving the 2% target," Muguruma says. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0603 GMT - Discomfort from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is "slowly amplifying," as the 10-year U.S. breakeven inflation rate is edging towards 2.5%, ING's Padhraic Garvey says in a note. "Far from catastrophic, but if it were to break above, it would be a growing problem, for both Treasuries and the Federal Reserve," the regional head of research, Americas, says in a note. The entire inflation curve shifted higher through Monday, in tandem with an edge higher in the price of oil, Garvey says. The 10-year inflation breakeven hit its highest level--at 2.45%--since the war broke, he says. "It's, in fact, been a tad surprising that longer tenor inflation had not seen a more marked rise so far." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0558 GMT - The Bank of Japan's 6-3 vote to keep the policy rate unchanged was interpreted by some market participants as a signal that the central bank will raise rates soon. While board members Hajime Takata and Naoki Tamura are considered supporters of tighter policy, Junko Nakagawa's opposition came as a surprise, as she is seen as more of a centrist. "Nakagawa's dissent may have been a strategic move to signal to the public that support for rate hikes is gaining traction across the board, rather than being confined to the usual two hawkish members," says Mizuho Securities economist Yusuke Matsuo. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0552 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields edge higher in a steepening manner--with short-end yields rising less than long-end yields--as oil prices climb in Asian trading. Treasury investors are focused on the Federal Reserve's coming meeting, where policy rates are expected to stay on hold. "All eyes will be on [Jerome] Powell's press conference, likely his last as Fed chair," says First Citizens Wealth's Phil Neuhart. "Markets are less focused on FOMC actions and more on the signal: is elevated inflation a transitory energy story or something more persistent?" the head of market and economic research says. The two-year Treasury yield is up 0.6 basis points at 3.809%, and the 10-year yield is 1.4 basis points higher at 4.349%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0547 GMT - Italian 10-year nominal government bond, or BTP, yields at around 3.80% remain well above last year's average and offer value from a carry perspective, says The Investment Institute by UniCredit's Luca Cazzulani in a note. "We continue to see current yield levels as attractive primarily from a carry perspective rather than for near‑term price appreciation," the head of strategy says. UniCredit expects the 10-year German Bund yield to remain around 3%, with the 10-year BTP-Bund yield spread stabilizing near current levels. The 10-year Italian BTP-German Bund yield spread closed at just below 80 basis points on Monday, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - The Federal Reserve's meeting will provide some ideas of those that favor reacting to energy-related inflation versus those that view it as transitory, Aptus Capital Advisors' John Luke Tyner says in a note. "But ultimately [it] won't make much difference," the head of fixed income says. A tamer April meeting, with no dot plots--Fed policymakers' quarterly forecasts of interest rates--and most likely no action, "does set the stage for fireworks in June where most likely [Kevin] Warsh will chair the meeting. A new dot plot is expected that month, and there will be more time for clarity on the Middle East situation and its implication on the economy and inflation, says Tyner, who is also portfolio manager. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Australian shares fall as U.S.–Iran impasse, inflation jitters curb risk appetite
data.description:
Key points:
  • AXJO closes at near-four week low, down 0.6%
  • Miners shed 0.9%
  • Energy stocks rebound to add 1.2%
  • Australia's March CPI data due on Wednesday

By Shruti Agarwal

Australian shares closed at their lowest in nearly four weeks on Tuesday as stalled U.S.-Iran peace talks and caution ahead of domestic inflation data kept investors on edge.

The S&P/ASX 200 index closed 0.6% lower at 8,710.70 points, its lowest finishing level since April 2. The benchmark has fallen over 2.5% in the last six straight losing sessions.

A sharp rise in oil prices following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has triggered one of Australia's biggest energy shocks in decades, raising concerns about inflation and economic growth.

Prolonged disruptions to energy flows could deepen concerns over slowing growth and persistent inflation, weighing on consumers and cyclical sectors, said William Taylor, COO and portfolio manager at ETF issuer etfshares.

Taylor added that weakness in the ASX 200 also reflected positioning ahead of March consumer inflation data due on Wednesday.

"Consensus expectations are for inflation to remain elevated year-on-year, and any upside surprise, particularly in the trimmed mean, would likely push out expectations for policy easing from the Reserve Bank of Australia."

Markets have priced in a more than 82% chance of a third cash rate hike at the central bank's May meeting.

Miners led losses, falling 0.9%, as copper prices extended declines and higher shipments weighed on iron ore prices. Heavyweights Rio Tinto and BHP lost 0.5% and 1.3%, respectively.

Gold stocks fell 2.7% as bullion weakened, hitting their lowest in three weeks.

Energy was the only sector to rise, gaining 1.2%.

Whitehaven Coal rose 3.9% after reporting higher quarterly output.

Healthcare fell 1.2%, while real estate (.AXRE) and consumer discretionary stocks (.AXDJ) lost 1.2% and 2.3%, respectively.

European Lithium surged as much as 63.2% to A$0.465, hitting its highest since October 15, after it received a buyout bid from Critical Metals.

New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index fell 0.9% to 12,764.40 points.

data.description:
